tree.mainmatter:
Given a pair of reflection algebras \(X,Y:{\mathcal{U}}\downarrow{A}\) and a pair of homomorphisms \(f,g : X\multimap Y\), a homotopy from \(f\) to \(g\) is defined to be a homotopy \(\phi : {Uf} \sim_{X\to Y} {Ug}\) between the underlying functions together with a witness \(\bar{\phi}\)  of the following coherence:\[   \bar\phi :
{\textstyle\prod_{a:A}}{  \eta_{g}a
} =_{  {g{\left(\eta_{X}a\right)}} =_{Y} {\eta_{Y}a}} {  \eta_{f}a\bullet \phi{\left(\eta_{X}a\right)}}\]
We shall write \({f} \sim_{X\multimap Y} {g}\) for the type of homotopies from the homomorphism \(f\) to the homomorphism \(g\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a subuniverse of \(\mathcal{V}\) and let \(A:\mathcal{V}\) be a type. A reflection algebra \(X\) under \(A\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) is defined to be a type \(UX:\mathcal{U}\) together with a function \({A}\xrightarrow{\eta_{X}}{UX}\). A homomorphism \(f\) of reflection algebras from \(X\) to \(Y\) is given by a function \({UX}\xrightarrow{Uf}{UY}\) together with a homotopy \(\eta_{f} : {\eta_{Y}} \sim_{A\to Y} {Uf\circ \eta_X}\).
When it causes no ambiguity, we will write \(X:\mathcal{U}\) for the underlying type \(UX\) of a given reflection algebra \(X\); likewise, we will write \(f x\) rather than \(Uf x\).

frontmatter.title: The Type of Reflection Algebras and Homomorphisms

mainmatter.p: We will write \({A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}} :\equiv {\textstyle\sum_{X:\mathcal{U}}}{A\to X}\) for the type of reflection algebras under \(A\); given two reflection algebras \(X,Y:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) we will write \({\left(X\multimap Y\right)} :\equiv {\textstyle\sum_{f:X\to Y}} {\eta_{Y}} \sim_{A\to Y} {f\circ \eta_{X}}\) for the type of homomorphisms of reflection algebras.

frontmatter.title: Identity and Composition of Reflection Algebra Homomorphisms

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be a reflection algebra; we define the identity homomorphism \(1_{X}:X\multimap X\) on \(X\) as follows:\[ \begin{array}{l} 1_{X}x :\equiv x \\ \eta_{1_{X}} a :\equiv \mathsf{refl}\end{array}\]
Now let \(Y,Z:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be two further reflection algebras and let \(f : X\multimap Y\) and \(g : Y\multimap Z\) be two homomorphisms of reflection algebras. The composite homomorphism \(f;g : X\multimap Z\) is defined like so:\[ \begin{array}{l} {\left(f;g\right)}x :\equiv g{\left(fx\right)} \\ \eta_{f;g}a :\equiv \mathsf{ap}_{g}\,{\left(\eta_{f}a\right)}\bullet \eta_{g}a \end{array}\]

Given a pair of reflection algebras \(X,Y:{\mathcal{U}}\downarrow{A}\) and a pair of homomorphisms \(f,g : X\multimap Y\), a homotopy from \(f\) to \(g\) is defined to be a homotopy \(\phi : {Uf} \sim_{X\to Y} {Ug}\) between the underlying functions together with a witness \(\bar{\phi}\)  of the following coherence:\[   \bar\phi :
{\textstyle\prod_{a:A}}{  \eta_{g}a
} =_{  {g{\left(\eta_{X}a\right)}} =_{Y} {\eta_{Y}a}} {  \eta_{f}a\bullet \phi{\left(\eta_{X}a\right)}}\]
We shall write \({f} \sim_{X\multimap Y} {g}\) for the type of homotopies from the homomorphism \(f\) to the homomorphism \(g\).

mainmatter.p: Given a path of reflection algebras \(X,Y:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) and a homomorphism \(f : X\multimap Y\), the type \({\textstyle\sum_{g:X\multimap Y}}{{g} \sim_{X\multimap Y} {f}}\) of homomorphisms equipped with a homotopy to \(f\) is contractible, by function extensionality and the characterization of path spaces in dependent sums. Hence it follows that the canonical map \({f} =_{X\multimap Y} {g}\to {f} \sim_{X\multimap Y} {g}\) is an equivalence.

tree.mainmatter: Given a type \(K\) and a family of reflection algebras \(X : K\to {A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that the dependent product \({\textstyle\prod_{k:K}}U{\left(Xk\right)}\) lies in \(\mathcal{U}\), we may define the product of product of reflection algebras \({\textstyle\prod_{k:K}}Xk\) to have the following structure map:\[ \begin{array}{l} \eta_{{\textstyle\prod_{k:K}}Xk} : A \to {\textstyle\prod_{k:K}}{Xk} \\ \eta_{{\textstyle\prod_{k:K}}Xk} a :\equiv \lambda k:K.\, \eta_{Xk}a \end{array}\]

mainmatter.p: Let \(X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) be a reflection algebra; a displayed reflection algebra \(Y\) over \(X\) is defined to be a dependent type \(\bar{U}Y : X \to \mathcal{U}\) together with a dependent function \(\bar{\eta}_{Y} : {\textstyle\prod_{a:A}}{\bar{U}Y{\left(\eta_{X}a\right)}}.\)

tree.mainmatter:
Given a displayed reflection algebra \(Y\) over a reflection algebra \(X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we may define the total reflection algebra \(\widetilde{Y}:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) of \(Y\) as follows:\[ \begin{array}{l} U\widetilde{Y} :\equiv {\textstyle\sum_{x:Ux}}Y[x] \\ \eta_{\widetilde{Y}}a :\equiv {\left(\eta_{X}a,\bar{\eta}_{Y}a\right)} \end{array}\]
The projection function \({U\widetilde{Y}}\xrightarrow{\pi_1}{UX}\) extends to a homomorphism of reflection algebras \(\pi_{Y}:\widetilde{Y}\multimap X\) as follows:\[ \begin{array}{l} \pi_{Y} {\left(x,y\right)} :\equiv x \\ \alpha_{\pi_{Y}} a :\equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]

frontmatter.title: Displayed Reflection Algebras Over Displayed Reflection Algebras

mainmatter.p: If \(Y\) is a displayed reflection algebra over a reflection algebra \(X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), we define a displayed reflection algebra over \(Y\) to be a displayed reflection algebra in the original sense over the total reflection algebra \(\widetilde{Y}\) corresponding to \(Y\).

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a subuniverse of \(\mathcal{V}\) and let \(A:\mathcal{V}\) be a type. A reflection algebra \(X\) under \(A\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) is defined to be a type \(UX:\mathcal{U}\) together with a function \({A}\xrightarrow{\eta_{X}}{UX}\). A homomorphism \(f\) of reflection algebras from \(X\) to \(Y\) is given by a function \({UX}\xrightarrow{Uf}{UY}\) together with a homotopy \(\eta_{f} : {\eta_{Y}} \sim_{A\to Y} {Uf\circ \eta_X}\).
When it causes no ambiguity, we will write \(X:\mathcal{U}\) for the underlying type \(UX\) of a given reflection algebra \(X\); likewise, we will write \(f x\) rather than \(Uf x\).

frontmatter.title: Splitting the Generic Element of the Generic Cone

tree.mainmatter:
The underlying function \(p_{I}:I\to I\) of the generic element \(p_{I}:I\multimap I\) of the generic cone \(p_{\bullet}:{\textstyle\prod_{X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}I\multimap X\) under \(I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) has been equipped with the structure of a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman, so we may split it.
In particular, the construction of Shulman gives us a type \(J\) together with a retraction \(r : I\to J\) and a section \(s : J \to I\) witnessed by a homotopy \(\rho:{s;r} \sim_{J\to J} {1_{J}}\), such that \(r;s\equiv p_{I} : I \to I\) definitionally. Our goal is to lift this splitting of the underlying function \(p_{I}:I\to I\) to a splitting of the homomorphism \(p_{I}:I\multimap I\).
We may equip \(J\) with the structure of a reflection algebra by setting \(\eta_{J} :\equiv \eta_{I};r\), making \(r : I \multimap J\) a homomorphism of algebras definitionally, i.e. \(\eta_{r}a:\equiv \mathsf{refl}\). To exhibit \(s : J \multimap I\) as a homomorphism of algebras, we may likewise use reflexivity:\[ \begin{array}{l} \eta_{s}:{\eta_{J};s} \sim_{A\to I} {\eta_{I}} \\ \eta_{s}a :\equiv \mathsf{refl}\end{array}\]
That the above is well-defined can be deduced as follows, using the fact that the splitting of \(p_{I}\equiv r;s\) is definitional and that \(p_{I}\) is a definitional homomorphism:\[ \begin{aligned} s{\left(\eta_{J}a\right)} & \equiv s{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)} \\ & \equiv p_{I}{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)} \\ & \equiv \eta_{I}a \end{aligned}\]
We have to check that the homotopy of functions \(\rho : {s;r} \sim_{J\to J} {1_{J}}\) lifts to a homotopy of homomorphisms. In particular, we must construct the coherence \(\bar\rho\) specified below:\[ \bar\rho : {\textstyle\prod_{a : A}}\eta_{1_{J}}a = \eta_{s;r}a \bullet \rho{\left(\eta_{J}a\right)}\]
Note that \(\eta_{1_{J}}\) is reflexivity; we compute the other side of the desired identificiation \(\bar\rho a\) by unfolding definitions (e.g. that of identities and compositions of homomorphisms) and using basic path algebra:\[ \begin{aligned} \eta_{s;r}a \bullet \rho{\left(\eta_{J}a\right)} & \equiv {\left(\mathsf{ap}_{r}{\left(\eta_{s}a\right)} \bullet \eta_{r}a\right)} \bullet \rho{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)} \\ & \equiv {\left(\mathsf{ap}_{r}\mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl}\right)} \bullet \rho{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)} \\ & \equiv {\left(\mathsf{refl}\bullet\mathsf{refl}\right)} \bullet \rho{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl}\bullet \rho{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)} \\ & = \rho{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)}\end{aligned}\]
Therefore, to construct \(\bar\rho a\) is suffices to identify \(\rho{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)}\) with reflexivity. To do so, we will use an argument suggested by Shulman in private conversation. We recall from Theorem 3.3 of Shulman that the homotopy \(\rho\) induces a new witness of pre-idempotency for \(p_{I}\), namely \(\chi' x :\equiv \mathsf{ap}_{s}{\left(\rho{\left(rx\right)}\right)}\) and that by Theorem 7.1 of the same, this homotopy may be identified with our original witness \(\chi : {p_{I};p_{I}} \sim_{I\to I} {p_{I}}\). Therefore we have \(\mathsf{ap}_{s}{\left(\rho{\left(rx\right)}\right)} = \vartheta_{x}p_{I}\); because \(s\) is a section of \(r\), this implies that \(\rho{\left(r x\right)} = \mathsf{ap}_{r}{\left(\vartheta_{x}p_{I}\right)}\) and so we may compute our identification \(\bar\rho\, a\) as follows:\[ \begin{aligned} \rho{\left(r{\left(\eta_{I}a\right)}\right)} & = \mathsf{ap}_{r}{\left(\vartheta_{\eta_{I}a}p_{I}\right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap}_{r} {\left(\eta_{p_{I}}a\right)} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap}_{r}\mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl}\end{aligned}\]
Above we have used the fact that the coherence datum \(\eta_{p_{I}}a\) is reflexivity.

frontmatter.title: Quasi-idempotence of the Generic Element of the Generic Cone

tree.mainmatter:
The generic element \(p_{I}:I\multimap I\) of the generic cone \(p_{\bullet}:{\textstyle\prod_{X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}I\multimap X\) under \(I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) can be equipped with the structure of a pre-idempotent. In particular, we may define a homotopy \(\chi : {p_{I};p_{I}} \sim_{I\multimap I} {p_{I}}\) using the incoherent naturality structure of any element of \(I\).\[ \begin{array}{l} \chi\,u :\equiv \vartheta_{u}p_{I} \\ \bar{\chi}a :\equiv \mathsf{refl}\end{array}\]
That the higher identification \(\bar\chi{a} : \eta_{p_{I}}a = \eta_{p_{I};p_{I}}\bullet \vartheta_{\eta_{I}a}p_{I}\) may be defined by reflexivity follows from the definition of \(\eta_{I}a\), the computation of composite homomorphisms, and the fact that \(\eta_{p_{I}}a\) is also defined by reflexivity; in particular, we compute:\[ \begin{aligned} \eta_{p_{I};p_{I}} \bullet \vartheta_{\eta_{I}a}p_{I} & \equiv {\left(\mathsf{ap}_{p_{I}}{\left(\eta_{p_{I}}a\right)}\bullet \eta_{p_{I}}a\right)}\bullet \eta_{p_{I}}a \\ & \equiv {\left(\mathsf{refl}\bullet\mathsf{refl}\right)}\bullet\mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl}\bullet\mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl}\end{aligned}\]
We will use the semicoherence structure associated to any element of \(I\) to exhibit the underlying function \(p_{I}:I\to I\) of the generic element as a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman. In particular, we must exhibit for each \(u:I\) an identification \(\psi : \mathsf{ap}_{p_{I}}{\left(\chi u\right)} = \chi{\left(p_{I}u\right)}\). Unfolding definitions, this means to identify \(\mathsf{ap}_{p_{I}}{\left(\vartheta_{u}p_{I}\right)}\) with \(\vartheta_{p_{I}u}p_{I}\).
From the semicoherence structure \(\varsigma_{u} : \mathsf{SemiCoh}[\pi_{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}u]\) we obtain the following identification:\[ \varsigma_{u}\,p_{I}\,p_{I}\,p_{I}\,\chi: \mathsf{ap}_{p_{I}}{\left(\vartheta_{u}p_{I}\right)}\bullet \vartheta_{u}p_{I}= \chi{\left(\epsilon_{u}I\right)}\bullet \vartheta_{u}p_{I}\]
Canceling \(\vartheta_{u}p_{I}\), unfolding the definition of \(\chi\), and recalling that \(p_{I}u \equiv\epsilon_{u}I\) by definition, we obtain the desired identification \(\mathsf{ap}_{p_{I}}{\left(\vartheta_{u}p_{I}\right)}=\vartheta_{p_{I}u}p_{I}\).
Therefore, the underlying function \(p_{I}:I\to I\) has the structure of a quasi-idempotent.

mainmatter.p: Given a path of reflection algebras \(X,Y:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) and a homomorphism \(f : X\multimap Y\), the type \({\textstyle\sum_{g:X\multimap Y}}{{g} \sim_{X\multimap Y} {f}}\) of homomorphisms equipped with a homotopy to \(f\) is contractible, by function extensionality and the characterization of path spaces in dependent sums. Hence it follows that the canonical map \({f} =_{X\multimap Y} {g}\to {f} \sim_{X\multimap Y} {g}\) is an equivalence.

tree.mainmatter:
Let \(\mathcal{U}\in\mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative universe pair with \(A:\mathcal{V}\) such that \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) is the displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures over the product \({\textstyle\prod_{X:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}}}X\) of all reflection algebras. We define a further displayed reflection algebra \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\mathsf{INatStr}\) of “semicoherence structures” associating to each incoherent naturality structure one dimension of coherence data for binary compositions of homomorphisms.
In particular, we define \(\mathsf{SemiCoh}\) over \(\widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}}\) as follows, employing our notion of homotopy between homomorphisms:\[ \begin{array}{l} \mathsf{SemiCoh}[u] :\equiv \\ \begin{array}{l} {\textstyle\prod_{{\left\{X,Y,Z:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\right\}}}} {\textstyle\prod_{f : X\multimap Y}} {\textstyle\prod_{g : Y\multimap Z}} {\textstyle\prod_{h : X \multimap Z}} {\textstyle\prod_{\phi : {f;g} \sim_{X\multimap Z} {h}}} \\ \mathsf{ap}_{g} {\left(\vartheta_{u} f\right)} \bullet \vartheta_{u} g = \phi{\left(\epsilon_{u} X\right)} \bullet \vartheta_{u} h \end{array}\end{array}\]
To define the displayed algebra structure \(\bar\eta_{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\), we will use the contractibility of singletons several times. In particular, given \({\left(Y,f\right)}\) where \(Y:{A}\downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) and \(f:X\multimap Y\), the algebra component \(\eta_{Y}\) is completely determined by the homotopy \(\eta_{f} :{\eta_{Y}} \sim_{A\to Y} {f \circ \eta_{X}}\); likewise, by our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the pair \({\left(h,\phi\right)}\) is uniquely determined. Therefore, we may define \(\bar\eta_{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) to be the unique function satisfying the following equation:\[\bar\eta_{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\,a\,{\left(f,\lambda\_.\mathsf{refl}\right)}\,{\left(g,\lambda\_.\mathsf{refl}\right)}\,{\left({\left(f;g\right)},\lambda\_.\mathsf{refl}\right)}, {\left(\lambda\_.\mathsf{refl},\lambda\_.\mathsf{refl}\right)} := \mathsf{refl}\]
Given an element \(u:\widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\), we shall write \(\epsilon_{u},\vartheta_{u}\) for the components of its underlying incoherent naturality structure \(\pi_{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}u\) and \(\varsigma_{u} : \mathsf{SemiCoh}[\pi_{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}u]\) for the semicoherence structure itself.
This definition is similar to that of Awodey, Frey, and Speight, except that we do not impose any coherence law for identities.
